title: Dog Limping Intermittently on Left Back Leg: Triage Guide
meta_desc: Is your dog limping on their left back leg? Learn about intermittent lameness, when to see a vet, and how photos help with veterinary triage.
content_html:
TL;DR: Intermittent limping in dogs is typically a non-emergency caused by conditions like a luxating patella or minor strains; monitor your pet for 24–48 hours and record a video of the limp to help your vet reach a diagnosis.

What does it mean if my dog has an intermittent limp in their back leg?

Intermittent limping, also known as episodic lameness, is when your dog favors a leg at certain times but appears to walk normally at others. When this occurs in a back leg, it often indicates a condition that fluctuates in severity, such as a luxating patella (a kneecap that slips out of place), early-stage arthritis, or a minor soft tissue strain. It is important to watch for patterns, such as whether the limp occurs after rest or after vigorous exercise.

Is my dog's intermittent limping considered a medical emergency?

  • The urgency level for an intermittent limp is generally low, as the dog is still able to bear weight on the limb at times.
  • It is typically safe to monitor your pet at home for 24 to 48 hours or wait for a scheduled veterinary appointment.
  • Contact an emergency clinic immediately if the limp becomes permanent, if your dog is unable to stand, or if they show signs of severe distress like panting and shaking.

How can a photo or video of the limp help my veterinarian with triage?

  • Capture a video of your dog walking at home; dogs often experience an adrenaline rush at the clinic that can temporarily hide a limp during a physical exam.
  • Take photos of the leg from various angles to help identify subtle swelling, paw pad injuries, or muscle wasting.
  • Visual documentation allows the veterinary team to see the gait abnormality in a relaxed environment, which is crucial for an accurate diagnosis.

Clinical Context (Merck Veterinary Manual)

Intermittent limping in a dog's left back leg necessitates a thorough orthopedic examination. Observation of gait abnormalities, such as a shortened stride and head drop during weight-bearing on the affected limb, is crucial. Palpation of the limb, proceeding from distal to proximal, should assess for swelling, pain, instability, crepitation, reduced range of motion, and muscle atrophy. Serial examinations before and after exercise may be needed for subtle cases. Diagnostic imaging, including radiography, ultrasonography, CT, or MRI, can aid in identifying the underlying cause. Lyme borreliosis and Anaplasma phagocytophilum infection, which can induce intermittent lameness, should be considered, along with other orthopedic disorders. Lumbosacral disease should also be ruled out.

Chapter: Infectious Diseases, Orthopedics

Source: The Merck Veterinary Manual, 11th Edition (Page 660)

faqs.question: Can I give my dog human aspirin for the limp?
faqs.answer: No, you should never give your dog human pain medication like aspirin, ibuprofen, or Tylenol, as these can be highly toxic. Only use medications prescribed by your veterinarian.

faqs.question: Should I rest my dog if they are limping intermittently?
faqs.answer: Yes, 'exercise restriction' is recommended. Avoid long walks, jumping on furniture, and rough play until your dog has been evaluated or the limp has completely resolved.

faqs.question: What are common causes of back leg limping?
faqs.answer: Common causes include Cranial Cruciate Ligament (CCL) tears, hip dysplasia, luxating patellas, or simply a thorn or cut on the paw pad.
